content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Nicole C. Wright 
Senior Deputy City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
500 Castro Street 
PO Box 7540 
Mountain View, CA 94039-7540 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-033 
Dear Ms. Wright: 
March 13, 2015 
This letter responds to your requests for advice on behalf of the City of Mountain View 
Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee Member Simon Purdon and Member Marc Roddin 
regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act m, a finder of 
fact when it provides advice. (/n re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Additionally, our advice applies only under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act 
and not under other genernl conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Government Code section I 090. 
QUESTION 
May Members Purdon and Roddin participate in governmental decisions relating to the 
California Street/Escuela Avenue Complete Street Feasibility Study? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Neither Members Purdon nor Roddin have a conflict of interest with respect to 
governmental decisions relating to the California Street/Escuela Avenue Complete Street 
Feasibility Study. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of lhe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulalory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-15-033 
Page No. 2 
Tire California Street/Escue/a Ave1tue Complete Street Feasibility Study ("Study"): 
The Study is intended to develop alternatives to improve the bicycling and pedestrian 
environment in the Study area. The Study area will include the California Street Corridor 
between Bryant Street and Showers Drive; Escuela A venue from Latham Street to Villa Street; 
and the intersection at Showers Drive. The Shoreline Boulevard segment is from Montecito to El 
Camino Real. 
The Study will analyze the California Street Corridor between Bryant Street and Showers 
Drive and develop alternatives for improvements to pedestrian sidewalks and crossings, bike 
lanes, and lighting. lmprovements, which may be included in the alternative concepts are curb 
bulb outs to reduce crossing width for pedestrians, increased areas for bicycles, and improved 
signage. For purposes of this Study, the California Street Corridor is defined as the area 
extending from downtown Mountain View (Bryant Street) to Showers Drive; Escuela Avenue 
from Latham Street to Villa Street; and the intersection at Showers Drive. The Shoreline 
Boulevard segment is from Montecito to El Camino Real. 
Currently, California Street is a four lane residential collector street between Showers 
Drive and Oak Street and two lane residential collector streets between Oak Street and Bryant 
Street that primarily includes multi-family residential with some commercial around Showers. 
Shoreline Boulevard is four lanes from Montecito Avenue to Wright Avenue and six lanes from 
Wright Avenue to El Camino Real. This segment is considered a residential collector from El 
Camino Real to Central Expressway and an avenue street north of Central Expressway. Shoreline 
Boulevard in this segment primarily includes multi-family residential, some single-family 
homes, a high school, and a fire station. Escuela Avenue is a two lane road that primarily 
includes multifamily residential with an elementary school. 
The Study may consider a potential "road diet" on portions or all of California Street and 
Shoreline Boulevard. This means the Study will consider vehicular lane elimination and/or 
narrowing for portions or all of California Street and Shoreline Boulevard, within the Study area. 
The Study will evaluate how bicycle and pedestrian paths are likely to be used on these street 
segments in order to reach various destinations, including Castro School, Senior Center, The 
View Teen Center, Community Center, and Caltrain Station. 
The Study will develop a conceptual design of a corridor for vehicles, pedestrians, and 
bicyclists in a safe environment. The potential traffic impacts of any vehicular lane eliminations 
or other potential bicycle and pedestrian improvements are unknown at this time. 
The underlying project does not pose any privacy or view considerations, and while the 
goal is greater pedestrian and bicycling activity, such increases in use are not likely to rise to the 
level of substantially altering the intensity of use to have an effect on real property values 


File No. A-15-033 
Page No. 3 
nearby. If the goal of greater pedestrian and bicycling activity is realized, and any road diet 
ultimately completed, it is unknown at this time whether these improvements may have an 
impact on air quality. 
The Bicycle alld Pedestrian Advisory Committee (Bf PAC): 
The B/P AC is an advisory committee to the City Council. Over an extended period of 
time, B/PAC's substantive recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by the City Council, and the City believes the members of B/P AC 
are public officials and that the conflict of interest rules apply. 
At the B/PAC meeting to be held on March 25, 2014, B/PAC will review draft concepts 
and provide input and comments to City Council regarding the alternatives to be included in the 
Study. After City Council approves the final concepts to include in the study, the Study will be 
finalized. The final Study will be used to direct future implementation for bicycle and pedestrian 
improvements in the Study area when funding is available. 
Bf PAC Members: 
• 
Bf PAC Member Purdon: Member Purdon owns a home within 466 feet from the project area 
at 1043 Villa Street, Mountain View. There is one residential street and numerous 
intervening residences between the Property and both Shoreline_ Boulevard and California 
Street. The street in between his Property and Shoreline Boulevard and California Street is 
considered a downtown street. 
A traffic study on the potential traffic improvements has not been conducted. In a 
preliminary review of the potential traffic impacts to Member Purdon' s property, the City 
considered whether a road diet, if adopted, would cause traffic diversion onto his street or 
parking issues. It is unlikely a road diet on California would cause parking issues, as there is 
currently no parking allowed on California from Shoreline Boulevard to Oak Street and only 
one lane of travel from Oak Street to Franklin Street (this is the case for both east and 
westbound traffic on California Street). Because there is no parking on California Street from 
Shoreline to Oak Street, any road diet imposed on California west of Shoreline Boulevard 
would unlikely create new parking issues. If a road diet is imposed on Shoreline Boulevard, 
parking should similarly not affect Member Purdon's property. Shoreline currently allows 
parking and has a bike lane. Any lane removal should not impact parking given the width of 
Shoreline Boulevard and current uses accommodated. 
• Member Roddin owns real property 1,330 feet from the project area. Several blocks separate 
his property from Shoreline Boulevard. Although a traffic impact analysis has not yet been 
performed, in a preliminary review of the potential traffic impacts to Member Rodd in' s 
property, the City considered whether a road diet, if adopted, would cause traffic diversion 
onto his street or parking issues. It is very unlikely any traffic would be diverted onto 
Member Roddin's street given that Shoreline Boulevard currently has three traffic lanes that 


File No. A-15-O33 
Page No. 4 
would be reduced to two. Given the size of the street, Shoreline could readily absorb a lane 
reduction. Moreover, Ernestine Lane is a small street with limited access points. It is far more 
likely that traffic would divert onto Miramonte or El Camino Real. Additionally, a road diet 
would likely not create any parking issues on Member Roddin' s street because of the 
distance from Shoreline Boulevard and the fact that the path to get to Ernestine Lane from 
Shoreline Boulevard in indirect. Finally, a road diet on Shoreline will likely have no impact 
on the existing parking on Shoreline. 
ANALYSIS 
ls it reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have a material 
financial effect 011 any of the public official'sfi11ancial interests? (Regulation 18701) 
Regulation 18701 provides that in cases where an official's interest is not explicitly 
involved in a decision,2 a financial effect is foreseeable if it is a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical.3 Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under 
which the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on real property is material. As relevant to your 
facts, the financial effect will be material if the decision: 
"( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property .... 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice 
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission 
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property." 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
! A financial effect on an interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmen1al decision before 1he official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of 
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental 
decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8705.2(a)( I )-(6). 
^ In contrast if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the 
public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered 
reasonably foreseeable. 


File No. A-15-033 
Page No. 5 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
As noted above, the B/PAC is considering the Study, which is limited to certain portions 
of three streets: California Street, Escuela Avenue and Shoreline Boulevard. B/PAC member 
Purdon owns a home within 466 feet from the portion of Shoreline Boulevard included in the 
Study. You note that a preliminary review of the potential improvements (improvements to 
pedestrian sidewalks and crossings, bike lanes, and lighting, possibly use of curb bulb outs and 
road diet) would not likely affect Member Purdon's property or street. 
With respect to Member Reddin, his property is even further away. Based on the nature 
of the decisions, improvements 1imited to existing bicycle lanes and pedestrian walkways on the 
primary routes of travel through the neighborhood, there is no reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effects on the values of any residential properties on the streets that connect to these 
primary routes. 
Having concluded that neither member has a conflict of interest, we would note that 
projects such as the one contemplated by the study tend to evolve and facts may change. If the 
facts do change you should contact us for further advice. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660 . 
. Wallace

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Members Purdon and Roddin participate in governmental decisions relating to the 
California Street/Escuela Avenue Complete Street Feasibility Study?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Neither Members Purdon nor Roddin have a conflict of interest with respect to 
governmental decisions relating to the California Street/Escuela Avenue Complete Street 
Feasibility Study. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of lhe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulalory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
